concepts_addressed: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels

frameworks.name: Four Investor Acquisition Channels
frameworks.definition: Four discrete paths to reach accredited investors without relying on a personal network: (1) affiliate model (leverage someone else's existing investor audience), (2) paid ads (targeted to accredited investors), (3) content marketing (thought leadership that attracts inbound investor interest), (4) outbound direct contact.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that founders seeking accredited investors outside their personal network have exactly four viable paths: the affiliate model (partner with someone who already has those investors), paid ads targeting accredited investors, content marketing, and outbound direct outreach. There are no other structurally distinct methods.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder does not want to use personal network

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that attending trade shows to find investors is structurally equivalent to the affiliate model — both involve paying a third party for access to their existing audience — and should be categorized under that channel rather than treated as a separate path.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that raising capital from accredited investors follows the same structural process as an enterprise sale — even though it feels different or quacks different — because the trust-building, qualification, and conversion mechanics are identical.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: the underlying process of qualifying a high-trust buyer and converting them is the same regardless of whether the product is a service or an investment vehicle

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in-person conferences and events are the highest-leverage starting channel for founders raising capital from accredited investors, primarily because face-to-face interaction compresses the time required to build sufficient trust to close, compared to digital-only channels.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels
conditions: founder lacks an existing large media or media-buying background
founder is starting from zero with no personal network to leverage
claims.mechanism: face-to-face interaction produces faster trust accumulation than digital channels because investors can directly assess credibility and alignment in person
bounded_by: If founder has a strong background in media buying, paid ads may be a preferable starting channel

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that speed-to-trust is materially faster in face-to-face investor interactions than in digital or remote channels, making in-person events a structurally superior early-stage capital-raising channel when the founder lacks an established investor audience.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels
claims.mechanism: direct in-person contact enables non-verbal trust signals (body language, presence, immediate Q&A) that digital channels cannot replicate, shortening the trust-building timeline

examples.text: A micro-development housing company doing $46M in revenue and targeting $500M asked Alex Hormozi how to attract high-net-worth accredited investors without using their personal network. Hormozi outlined the four available channels (affiliate, paid ads, content, outbound), noted trade shows collapse into the affiliate bucket, then recommended conferences and events as the highest-leverage starting point because investor relationships are enterprise-grade and face-to-face trust builds fastest. He added the caveat that a founder with media-buying experience might reasonably start with paid ads instead.
examples.topic: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels

practices.text: When raising capital from accredited investors without a personal network, start with conferences and events before other channels unless you already have a background in media buying.
practices.topic: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels

practices.text: Treat an investor acquisition funnel as an enterprise sale: apply the same trust-building, qualification, and closing mechanics regardless of whether the product is a service or an investment vehicle.
When evaluating capital-raising channels, classify trade shows under the affiliate model (paying for access to someone else's audience) rather than as a standalone channel.
